abstract: While some scholars suggest that decolonial and decentring approaches are complementary—with the former opening political spaces for the latter to implement pragmatic, systemic evolutions—this synergy is far from guaranteed. Such alignment is particularly unlikely if the two agendas remain conceptually conflated. Although decentring may enhance the legitimacy and efficacy of EU foreign policy, it risks maintaining, or even reinforcing, the underlying structures of colonial power. Consequently, research and policy must shift beyond the mere objective of "decentring Europe" toward decolonial futures that explicitly prioritize the democratic dismantling of colonial continuities.
